question_structure.context: As shown in the figure, a wooden board $B$ is placed on a horizontal ground, and a wooden block $A$ is placed on top of $B$. The right end of $A$ is fixed to a vertical wall by a horizontal light spring scale. A force $F=17\mathrm{N}$ is applied to pull $B$ to the left, causing it to move at a constant velocity. At this time, the reading of the spring scale remains constant at $F_{0}=8\mathrm{N}$. The masses of objects $A$ and $B$ are $m_{A}=4\mathrm{kg}$ and $m_{B}=5\mathrm{kg}$ respectively, and $g=10\mathrm{N/kg}$.
question_structure.sub_question_1: The coefficient of friction between $A$ and $B$.
question_structure.sub_question_2: The coefficient of friction between $B$ and the ground.

sub_question_1.step_1: Since the wooden board is moving at a constant speed and block $A$ is stationary, the magnitude of the sliding friction force on block $A$ is equal to the spring force: $f=F_{0}=\mu m_{A}g=8\mathrm{N}$. Solving for $\mu$, we get $\mu=\frac{8}{4\times10}=0.2$.

sub_question_2.step_2: Since the board is moving to the left at a constant velocity, the force analysis on the board in the horizontal direction yields $F=\mu m_{A}g+\mu^{\prime}(m_{A}+m_{B})g$. Solving for the coefficient of friction between board $B$ and the ground, we get $\mu^{\prime}=\frac{F-\mu m_{A}g}{m_{A}+m_{B}}=0.1$.

image_captions: A diagram shows a rectangular block, B, being pulled to the left by a force, F. A smaller block, A, rests on top of block B and is connected to a spring fixed to the right.

step_1.physical_theorem: Equilibrium of forces (static friction equals spring force) and sliding friction formula

result_quantity.name: Sliding friction force on block A
Coefficient of friction between A and B

step_2.physical_theorem: Equilibrium of forces (dynamic equilibrium) and sliding friction formula

result_quantity.name: Coefficient of friction between B and the ground
